title: Recent progress on Serre weight conjectures
speaker: Bao Le Hung
abstract: I will discuss some recent results on Serre weight conjectures in dimension $> 2$, based on the study of certain tame type deformation rings. This is joint work with (various subset of) D. Le, B. Levin and S. Morra.

proof_stages.label: Classical Serre conjecture and its weight part
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by recalling Serre's weak and strong conjectures for odd two-dimensional mod-p Galois representations of G_Q, emphasizing the local inertial recipe that predicts the possible small modular weights.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological reformulation of modular weights
proof_stages.summary: Serre weights are rephrased representation-theoretically as irreducible mod-p local systems whose Hecke eigenclasses occur in the cohomology of modular curves, leading to the general problem for locally symmetric spaces attached to reductive groups.

proof_stages.label: Compact-at-infinity simplification
proof_stages.summary: The speaker restricts to groups compact modulo center at infinity so that the relevant locally symmetric space is zero-dimensional and the cohomology becomes the space of algebraic automorphic forms.

proof_stages.label: Guiding principles from Galois representations and mod-p Langlands
proof_stages.summary: The expected answer is constrained by the Galois representation attached to a mod-p Hecke eigensystem, by a conjectural local-global compatible mod-p Langlands correspondence, and by p-adic Hodge-theoretic restrictions coming from crystalline lifts.

proof_stages.label: Known GL_2-type Serre weight conjectures
proof_stages.summary: For forms of GL_2, the Buzzard-Diamond-Jarvis/Jarvis-type conjectures give local Serre weight sets depending on tame inertia or crystalline lifts, and these conjectures are now essentially known.

proof_stages.label: Herzig's higher-rank weight recipe
proof_stages.summary: For unramified higher-rank groups and tame semisimple local Galois representations, Herzig's conjecture defines the local Serre weight set by reducing Deligne-Lusztig representations mod p, applying an involution, and isolating a tractable collection of 'obvious' weights amid a rapidly growing combinatorial set.

proof_stages.label: New progress for unitary groups
proof_stages.summary: Under Taylor-Wiles-type big image, tame semisimplicity, genericity, and mild ramification hypotheses for a compact unitary group split at p, the announced results prove Herzig's conjecture for n=3, prove one inclusion for general n, and show that modularity of one obvious weight implies modularity of all obvious weights.

proof_stages.label: Patching and local deformation-ring strategy
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy uses Taylor-Wiles patching functors to detect exactly which Serre weights occur, reducing nonvanishing questions for patched modules to the geometry of potentially crystalline local Galois deformation rings.

examples.name: Split versus nonsplit reducible inertia in Serre's recipe
examples.note: The speaker spells out the case where the restriction to inertia is an extension of two characters, illustrating how Serre's local recipe gives one small weight in the nonsplit case and two in the split case.
examples.engagement_reason: he concretely applies Serre’s weight recipe to a reducible inertia representation, distinguishing split and nonsplit extensions and saying how many small weights result

examples.name: Split GL_n/Q_p local model for Herzig's recipe
examples.note: The case of a group locally isomorphic to GL_n over Q_p, even GL_3, is used to make the abstract higher-rank recipe concrete in terms of tame inertial data and Deligne-Lusztig representations.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes Herzig’s recipe to the split local group GL_n/Q_p, classifies tame semisimple representations by combinatorial data, identifies the principal-series/Deligne–Lusztig representation, and describes taking its mod-p Jordan–Hölder factors

examples.name: Diagonal sum of cyclotomic characters
examples.note: The representation given by a direct sum of powers of the cyclotomic character illustrates how inertial weights are encoded by a tuple and later how the 'obvious' Serre weights arise from crystalline lifts.
examples.engagement_reason: he works out the diagonal cyclotomic-character case by identifying the inertial weights, writing the obvious Serre weights via permutations, and explaining the corresponding crystalline lifts by adding multiples of p−1
